title: Regulator-portal submission evidence — one-way API acknowledgement audit
exclusions: Does NOT validate correctness of the report content; pairs with internal QA review.
evidence: Hourly search 'regulator_submission_ack' captures submission → acknowledgement round-trip and stores receipts to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.33 requires the notification to reach the authority; a submitted-without-ack outcome is not a completed notification.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.33 (Breach notification to supervisory authority) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.39.2: Regulator-portal submission evidence — one-way API acknowledgement audit.

controlTest.positiveScenario: When a regulator submission is sent but no acknowledgement is captured within 15 minutes of submission, the UC fires and escalates to the incident coordinator.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Submissions with a valid tracking_id and an ack event do NOT fire.

dataSources: See subcategory preamble. — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
index=notifications sourcetype=regulator:submission earliest=-72h
| stats values(tracking_id) as tracking_id min(_time) as submitted by incident_id regulator
| join type=outer incident_id regulator [search index=notifications sourcetype=regulator:ack earliest=-72h | stats values(tracking_id) as ack_tracking_id max(_time) as acked by incident_id regulator]
| where isnull(ack_tracking_id) OR (acked-submitted)>900
| table incident_id regulator tracking_id submitted acked
description: Closes the loop on regulator submissions by requiring a matching ack. Silent failures of the submission channel show up within minutes instead of being noticed during audit months later.
value: Addresses the repeatedly-cited 'we submitted but the authority never acknowledged' defence that now carries no weight in supervisory decisions.
implementation: (1) Instrument submission and ack channels to emit matching tracking_ids; (2) schedule hourly; (3) on miss, SOAR opens an incident-notification retry case. Indexes required: `index=notifications`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=regulator:ack`, `sourcetype=regulator:submission`.
visualization: Funnel (submitted → acked), bar chart of delays by regulator, single-value 'submissions acked within 15 min'.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `notifications` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.33`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.33 (Breach notification to supervisory authority) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.39.2: Regulator-portal submission evidence — one-way API acknowledgement audit." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `notifications` under `regulator:submission`, `regulator:ack`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Three operational considerations, authored alongside this UC, apply:


• **Hint 1:** Instrument submission and ack channels to emit matching tracking_ids. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** schedule hourly. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** on miss, SOAR opens an incident-notification retry case. Automated remediation must be gated behind a defined approval workflow; compliance controls that take destructive action without human review are themselves a governance risk.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Regulator-portal submission evidence — one-way API acknowledgement audit," the aggregation is the evidence artifact itself — auditors will read these rows as the official record of GDPR control activity.

This search correlates the outer dataset (index=`notifications`, sourcetype=`regulator:submission`) with a subsearch on `incident_id regulator` across the last 72 hours. `type=outer` (left-outer) returns every row from the outer search and fills nulls where the inner search has no match. Use this when the compliance question is 'for every row in A, what does B say?' — the null pattern is itself the finding (e.g. 'ticket opened but no PIR recorded').

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.39.2: Regulator-portal submission evidence — one-way API acknowled]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -72h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.39.2,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=notifications sourcetype=regulator:submission earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `ack_tracking_id`, `acked`, `incident_id`, `regulator`, `regulator]`, `submitted`, `tracking_id`. Run:

```spl
index=notifications sourcetype=regulator:submission earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table ack_tracking_id, acked, incident_id, regulator, regulator], submitted, tracking_id
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.39.2: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Funnel (`submitted → acked`):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Bar Chart Of Delays By Regulator (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single-Value 'Submissions Acked Within 15 Min' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We help you see when incident clocks are tight so you can meet notification duties even after hours.
